perturbation.original_text: F. "Licensed Products" shall mean BlackMP Living Water, BlackMP Concentrate, Zezel Probiotic Water, Zayin Sports Water, Gridiron MVP™ and Gridiron MVP™ Concentrate using the Pro Football Legends Logo on the Licensed Products' affixed labels, hang-tags or packaging. Other products of the Company may be added to the list of Licensed Products during the Contract Period by written amendment to this Agreement. All amendments to this Agreement must be signed by all parties to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: F. "Licensed Products" shall mean BlackMP Living Water, BlackMP Concentrate, Zezel Probiotic Water, Zayin Sports Water, Gridiron MVP™ and Gridiron MVP™ Concentrate using the Pro Football Legends Logo on the Licensed Products' affixed labels, hang-tags or packaging. Other products of the Company may be added to the list of Licensed Products during the Contract Period by verbal agreement. Amendments to this Agreement may be oral or implied through conduct.
perturbation.explanation: This modification allows for verbal agreements to modify the contract, which contradicts standard contract law requiring written amendments, especially for agreements of this magnitude. It creates uncertainty regarding which products are licensed and under what terms.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Frauds - California Civil Code § 1624
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1624 requires certain contracts, including those not performable within a year, to be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. This modification makes it possible to alter a multi-year agreement verbally, directly contradicting the Statute of Frauds.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1624 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a lon
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows for verbal modifications to the contract, directly contradicting California Civil Code § 1624(a)(1), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that agreements not performable within a year must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. The original contract likely extends beyond one year, and the modification removes the requirement for written amendments, creating a clear contradiction. The law snippet explicitly states that such contracts are invalid unless in writing.

perturbation.original_text: Past due payments under this Agreement shall bear interest at the rate of: (a) 1% per month; or (b) the maximum interest rate permissible under law, whichever is less.
perturbation.changed_text: Past due payments under this Agreement shall bear interest at the rate of: (a) 25% per year; or (b) the maximum interest rate permissible under law, whichever is less.
perturbation.explanation: This change sets a high fixed interest rate that may exceed the legal limit and contradicts usury laws, which are designed to protect borrowers from excessive interest rates. It creates uncertainty about the enforceable interest rate.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Usury Law - California Constitution Article XV Section 1
perturbation.law_explanation: California Constitution Article XV Section 1 limits the interest rate for loans primarily for personal, family, or household purposes to 10% per annum or 5% plus the Federal Reserve rate. While this agreement is a commercial agreement, a rate of 25% per year is quite high and may be construed as unconscionable. This creates a contradiction with the intent of the law to protect parties from abusive lending practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a potentially low interest rate (1% per month) with a fixed high rate (25% per year). According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, Article XV, Section 1 of the California Constitution limits interest rates to a maximum of 10% per annum for loans primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. While the Law Explanation notes this is a commercial agreement, a 25% rate creates a high risk of violating usury laws, especially if the loan could be construed as for personal, family, or household purposes. The original clause included a provision to limit the rate to the maximum permissible under law, which the modified clause retains. However, setting a high fixed rate creates a clear contradiction as it risks violating the stated intent and the law. The change thus introduces a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction because the specified rate may exceed legally allowable rates.

perturbation.original_text: In no event will NFLA be liable for any indirect, incidental, reliance, special or consequential damages arising out of the performance or nonperformance of this Agreement, whether or not NFLA had been advised of the possibility of such damages.
perturbation.changed_text: In no event will NFLA be liable for any damages arising out of the performance or nonperformance of this Agreement, regardless of foreseeability.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the qualifiers 'indirect, incidental, reliance, special or consequential', this clause now attempts to exclude liability for all damages, even direct damages resulting from gross negligence or willful misconduct. This contradicts the legal principle that parties cannot contract away liability for their own intentional or grossly negligent actions, making the clause potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1668 states that contracts that exempt someone from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury, or violation of law are against public policy. This modified clause, attempting to waive all liability regardless of fault, conflicts with this provision and could be deemed unenforceable to the extent it attempts to shield NFLA from such conduct.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1668 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the qualifiers 'indirect, incidental, reliance, special or consequential' from the damages for which NFLA will not be liable. This attempts to waive all liability, even for direct damages resulting from gross negligence or willful misconduct. California Civil Code § 1668, as quoted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that contracts that exempt someone from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury, or violation of law are against public policy. The modified clause conflicts with this provision by attempting to shield NFLA from such conduct, thus creating a legal contradiction.
